Facts.text: During the Maccabean Revolt Judas Maccabeus recaptures Jerusalem and rededicates the Second Temple, commemorated since as Jewish festival Hanukkah
St Anterus begins his reign as Catholic Pope, will only rule for 40 days
The Institutes published - an official textbook of Roman law, part of Roman Emperor Justinian's program of legal reforms
Pope Sergius names Willibrord as Archbishop Clemens of Friezen
Frederik of Sierck/Zyrick becomes bishop of Utrecht
Richard Johnson, a free black, granted 550 acres in Virginia
J Vanbrughe's "Relapse or Virtue in Danger" premieres in London
Battle at Maxen: Prussian army surrenders to Austrians
Jean-François Pilâtre de Rozier and Marquis d'Arlandes make 1st manned free balloon flight in a Montgolfier balloon
North Carolina ratifies constitution, becomes 12th US state
Honolulu Harbor discovered
Stettin surrenders to allied armies
US soldiers attack Miccosukee Tribe village of Fowltown, Georgia, beginning what becomes known as the First Seminole War
First Jewish Reform congregation forms, Charleston, South Carolina
Giacomo Meyerbeer's opera "Robert le diable" (Robert the Devil) premieres at the Paris Opéra
HMS Beagle anchors at Bay of San Carlos, Chile
Thomas Morris of Australia skips rope 22,806 times
Steamer "Phoenix" is lost on Lake Michigan, kills 200
Alfred de Musset's "Andre del Sarto" premieres in Paris
Cincinnati Turngemeinde founded
Friedrich Hebbel's play "Der Rubin" premieres in Vienna
Duke University founded in 1838 as Union Institute, chartered as Normal College
Two-day Battle at Griswoldville, Georgia begins
Shaw University forms in Raleigh NC
Moses F Gale patents a cigar lighter (NYC)
Skirmish between HM Stanley's expedition & natives
1st night football game, Philadelphia Athletics beats Kanaweola AC, 39-0
Baseball's Philadelphia Athletics & Phillies form pro football teams, joining Pitts Stars in 1st attempt at a National Football League
The Canadian government appoints a commission to consider revising, classifying, and consolidating the many public statutes passed over the years
1st match ever played in the Australian National Tennis Championships
China prohibits the opium trade
Billy Mallett of Hamilton Tigers kicks 9 singles in a game
British army conquers Bazra
HMHS Britannic sinks in the Aegean Sea after a mine explodes, killing 30 people
2 German ammunition trains explode in Hamont Belgium, 1,750 die
Polish soldiers organize a pogrom against Jews of Galicia, Poland
The German High Seas Fleet of 5 battlecruisers, 9 battleships, 7 cruisers and 49 destroyers surrendered to the British Grand Fleet and were shepherded into the Firth of Forth
The trial of the accused of the Bulhoek Massacre commences in South Africa
Rebecca L Felton (Ga) sworn in as first female US Senator
1st US ambassador to USSR, W.C. Bullitt, begins service
"Uiver" returns from Schiphol in London-Melbourne air race
Jean Giraudoux' "La Guerre de Troie n'Aura" premieres in Paris
Belgian king Leopold III, King of the Belgians begins 3-day visit to Netherlands
Nazi forces occupy western Czechoslovakia and declare inhabitants to be German citizens
Nazi occupiers forbid building schools in Netherlands
German troops occupy Rostov-on-Don, Southern Russia
7 Belgian ministers in London criticise King Leopold III for surrendering to Germany
WWII: German submarine U-538 sunk on southwest of Ireland in the Atlantic Ocean by British warships
Personnel & executive staff of Philips demonstrate for more food
General Motors workers go on strike
Georgi Dimitrov elected premier of Bulgaria
Harry Truman becomes 1st US President to travel in a submerged sub
1st US postage stamp in 2 colors (rotary process) introduced
WKJG TV channel 33 in Ft Wayne, IN (NBC) begins broadcasting
KTVO TV channel 3 in Ottumwa-Kirksville, IA (ABC) begins broadcasting
Don Newcombe wins NL MVP & 1st-ever Cy Young Award
"La Ronde", 1st revolving restaurant in US, opens atop the 23-floor Ala Moana Tower in Honolulu, Hawaii; designed by architect John Graham, Jr.
The Chinese People's Liberation Army declares a unilateral cease-fire in the Sino-Indian War.
Verrazano-Narrows suspension bridge opens in New York City, then the world's longest
1st freighter arrives in Ashdod Port, Israel
USSR performs nuclear test at Eastern Kazakh/Semipalitinsk, USSR
Dutch government of Zijlstra forms
Phillip & Jay Kunz fly a kite a record 28,000 feet
Cin trades shortstop Leo Cardenas to Twins for pitcher Jim Merritt
KXIX (now KVCT) TV channel 19 in Victoria, TX (ABC) 1st broadcast
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
NY Knicks 1st game against Cleveland Cavaliers, Knicks win 102-94 at Madison Square Garden
Battle of Garibpur: Indian troops aided by the Mukti Bahini, Bengali guerrillas, defeat the army of Pakistan
NY Rangers scores a NHL record 8 goals in 1 period
Richard Baker becomes teacher of San Francisco Zen Center
MLB Boston Red Sox Carlton Fisk wins AL Rookie of Year, New York Mets Jon Matlack wins NL
US PresidentRichard Nixon's attorney, J. Fred Buzhardt, reveals presence of 18½ minute gap in a White House tape recording related to Watergate
Birmingham pub bombings: 21 civilians killed when bombs explode at two pubs in Birmingham, England (deadliest attack in England during "the Troubles")
"All 'N All" 8th studio album by Earth, Wind & Fire is released (Billboard Album of the Year 1978)
Atlanta Braves infielder Bob Horner wins NL Rookie of Year Award
Crowd at Islamabad, Pakistan attack US embassy, 1 dies
"Hi Infidelity" 9th studio album by REO Speedwagon is released
Fire at MGM Grand Hotel in Las Vegas kills 84
Gene Michael named 25th New York Yankees manager, replacing Dick Howser, who resigns
TV show "Dallas" episode "Who Done It" reveals 'Who Shot J.R.?', gets a then record 53.3 rating (over 83 million viewers, 76% of television watchers) in the US
400,000 demonstrate in Amsterdam against cruise missiles
Central African Republic adopts constitution
Suriname army leader Desi Bouterse massacres Moiwana village
TV cameras permitted in British House of Commons
Poetess Maria Elene Cruz Varela arrested in Cuba
Jan Karlsson swims world record 50m butterfly (23.80 sec)
Jani Sievinen swims world record 100m medley (53.78 sec)
Louise Karlsson swims world record 50m freestyle (31.19 sec)
Neo-fascist MSI wins 36% of municipal elections in Rome
Dow Jones Industrial Average closes above 5,000 for 1st time
Israel grants jailed US spy Jonathan Pollard citizenship
"Rehearsal" opens at Criterion Theater NYC
A propane explosion at the Humberto Vidal shoe store and office building in San Juan, Puerto Rico kills 33.
Canadian Royal Commission on Aboriginal Peoples says relationship between indigenous people and non-indigenous people should be completely re-structured
Elian Gonzalez, Cuban boy at the center of a heated 2000 controversy involving the governments of Cuba and the United States, departs from Cuba with his mother
NATO invites Bulgaria, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, Slovakia and Slovenia to become members.
Island of Dominica hit by the most destructive earthquake in its history. Damage concentrated in the north and the town of Portsmouth. Also felt in neighboring Guadeloupe, where one person is killed.
The Paris Club agrees to write off 80% (up to $100 billion) of Iraq's external debt.
The second round of the Ukrainian presidential election is held, unleashing massive protests and controversy over the election's integrity.
Anti-Syrian Lebanese Minister and MP Pierre Gemayel is assassinated in suburban Beirut.
Daughtry release their debut album “Daughtry” (2007 Billboard Album of the Year, American Music Awards Favorite Pop-Rock Album 2007)
An Israel and Hamas ceasefire is negotiated
Chelsea Manager Roberto Di Matteo is sacked and replaced by Rafael Benítez
31 people are killed, and dozens more injured during a truck bomb attack of an open-air vegetable market in Sadiyah, in northeastern Iraq
54 people are killed after the roof of a supermarket collapses in Riga, Latvia
The Alabama parole board grants posthumous pardons to three members of the Scottsboro boys
India celebrates 50th anniversary of IR8, rice variety specially bred that saved much of Asia from famine
CBS TV host Charlie Rose is fired after allegations of sexual harassment by eight women
Mt Agung on the Indonesian island of Bali begins erupting
Pixar animation head John Lasseter takes 6 month leave of absence amid sexual misconduct claims, leave eventual becomes permanent
Former Guatemalan soldier Santos López Alonzo sentenced to 5,160 years for killing 171 people in Dos Erres during the civil war
RIBA’s best building in the world biennial international prize awarded to Canuanã school in Brazil
22 killed in displacement camp in attack by Syrian government in Idlib province, Syria
44% of Americans work in low-wage jobs with medium annual wage of just $18,000, with most aged 25 - 54 according to analysis by Brookings Institution's Metropolitan Policy Program
First of a series of national strikes in Colombia against the government of President Iván Duque and proposed cuts to pensions
WHO says Measles has killed more than 5,000 people in Democratic Republic of Congo in 2019, in world's largest and fastest-moving epidemic
Texas National Guard mobilized to help El Paso County, deal with a morgue crisis as COVID-19 cases and deaths surge
"Voiceless Mass" by Raven Chacon, premieres at the Cathedral of St. John the Evangelist in Milwaukee - first work by an Native American to win the Pulitzer Prize for Music
China reports new COVID-19 outbreaks, with 28,127 new cases, with half in Guangzhou and the municipality of Chongqing and public venues closed in Beijing and Shanghai
Magnitude 5.6 earthquake strikes near the city of Cianjur, on the Indonesian island of Java, killing at least 268 people, with over 1000 injured
Changpeng Zhao, founder and CEO of Binance, world's largest cryptocurrency exchange, pleads guilty to allowing customers to launder money - will pay $4.3 billion in fines to US government
Controversial artwork of a banana duct-taped to a wall by Maurizio Cattelan sells for $6.2 million at auction in New York; new owner Justin Sun says he will eat it
Evidence presented of the earliest known alphabet, on clay cylinders, 4,400 years old, 500 years older than previous writing, discovered in tombs in Umm el-Marra, Syria
Future US presidentAndrew Jacksonadmitted to the bar aged 20
The Continental System declared in the Decree of Berlin: EmperorNapoleonI bans all trade with Britain
Russia'sTsar Alexander Ipetitions for a Jewish state in Palestine
Competing explorersHenry Morton Stanley, in the service of Belgian KingLeopold IIand Italian-French explorerPierre Savorgnan de Brazza, working for France, meet in the lower Congo
Edison lab records the 1st surviving motion picture, "Monkeyshines No. 1", shot by William am Kennedy Dickson and William Heise [date disputed between June 1889 and November 21–27, 1890]
Richard Strauss' opera "Feuersnot" premieres in Dresden
Maxim GorkycallsVladimir Lenina blind fanatic and unthinking adventurer
Karel Čapek's play "Vec Makropulos" premieres in Prague
Mussolini's squad begins terror, 11 die in Bologna, Italy
Silent film "The Last of the Mohicans" released, adapted fromJames Fenimore Cooper's novel, starringWallace Beery, Barbara Bedford
British Prime MinisterStanley Baldwincancels Labour contract with USSR
Red Grangeplays final Univ of Illinois game, signs with Chicago Bears
Horror film "Frankenstein" is released, starringBoris Karloffas the monster, directed by James Whale and based onMary Shelley's 1818 novel "Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus"
NY Yankees buyJoe DiMaggiofrom SF Seals (Pacific Coast League)
Dmitri Shostakovich's 5th Symphony premieres in Lenningrad, with the ovation lasting over an hour
Adolf Hitlernames field marshal Erich von Manstein commander of the newly-created Army Group Don (Heeresgruppe Don)
Benjamin Britten's String Quartet No. 2 premieres at the Wigmore Hall, London, played by the Zorian Quartet
"The Best Years of Our Lives", film directed byWilliam Wyler, based on MacKinlay Kantor's novella Glory for Me, starringMyrna LoyandFredric March, is released (Academy Awards Best Picture 1947)
Bill Longson beatsLou Theszin St Louis, to win National Wrestling Association World Heavyweight title
Bill Veecksells MLB Cleveland Indians for $22 million, to fund his divorce settlement
Argentina asks Panama for return of ex-president Juan Peron
EntertainerJack Benny(violin) and US Vice-PresidentRichard Nixon(piano) play their famed duet
Bob Scheffing signs to manage Tigers afterCasey Stengelturns it down
US PresidentJohn F. Kennedyflies to Texas (assassinated the next day)
PopePaul VIsigns 3rd sitting of 2nd Vatican council
Vietnam War: American GeneralWilliam Westmorelandtells news reporters: "I am absolutely certain that whereas in 1965 the enemy was winning, today he is certainly losing."
The Supremes& The Temptations release "I'm Gonna Make You Love Me"
GeneralHafez al-Assadbecomes Prime Minister of Syria following a military coup
Pete Rose(Cincinnati Reds) wins MLB's NL MVP
Amendments to the Freedom of Information Act broadening public access to US government actions passed by Congress over PresidentGerald Ford's veto
Linda McCartneydrug charges in US are dropped
"Rocky" directed by John G. Avildsen and starringSylvester Stallonepremieres in New York (Best Picture 1977)
Baltimore Orioles 1st basemanEddie Murraywins AL Rookie of Year
John LennonandYoko Onopose nude for photographer Allan Tannenbaum
Olivia Newton-John's "Physical" single goes #1 & stays for 10 weeks
Law banning smoking on most domestic flights signed by US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bush
French PresidentFrançois Mitterrandvoices support for a proposed UN resolution that would authorize the use of force in the Persian Gulf
Michael Milkenis sentenced to 10 years for security law violations
Oregon SenatorBob Packwoodissues apology for unwelcome sexual advances
German tennis star Michael Stich scores a 7–6, 2–6, 7–6, 6–2 win over AmericanPete Samprasto win his first and only ATP Tour World Championship title in Frankfurt, Germany
Top seeded German tennis playerSteffi Grafwins WTA Tour Championship; beatsArantxa Sánchez Vicarioof Spain 6–1, 6–4, 3–6, 6–1 at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Columbia Records releasesBruce Springsteen's 11th studio album "The Ghost of Tom Joad"; wins Grammy Award for best Contemporary Folk Album
Repeat of previous year's final, but different result; American tennis starLindsay DavenportbeatsMartina Hingis6-4, 6-2 for the WTA Tour Championship at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Roger Federerof Switzerland wins back-to-back season-ending Tennis Masters Cup titles with a 6–3, 6–2 victory over Australian Leyton Hewitt in the final in Houston, Texas
"Kingdom Come" 9th studio album byJay-Zis released
"Happy" single is released byPharrell Williams(Billboard Song of the Year 2014, Grammy Award for Best Pop Solo Performance 2015)
Adam Ondracompletes the second free ascent of The Dawn Wall at El Capitan, leading every pitch in 8 days
Elon Musklaunches Tesla's electric Cybertruck with shatterproof windows that when demonstrated on stage shatter
Alexander Zverevof Germany captures his second ATP Finals men's tennis title defeating world #2Daniil Medvedev6-4, 6-4 in Turin
British driverLewis Hamiltonwins 1st Qatar Grand Prix Formula One motor race at the Losail International Circuit in Qatar
Bradley Cooper's film "Maestro" about conductorLeonard Bernstein's marriage to actress Felicia Montealegre is released in US
MLB Los Angeles DodgersShohei Ohtani(NL) and New York YankeesAaron Judge(AL) both unanimously chosen as winners of their league MVP
Authorities at the British Natural History Museum announce the "Piltdown Man" skull, one of the most famous fossil skulls in the world, is a hoax
Robert Mugabe's resignation after 37 years in power is read out in Zimbabwe's parliaments during impeachment proceedings
John Bale, English bishop and anti-catholic playwright (Kynge Johan), born in Cove, Suffolk, England (d. 1563)
Charles Scribani, Flemish Jesuit and author, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1629)
Anne de Xainctonge, French saint and founder of the Society of the Sisters of Saint Ursula of the Blessed Virgin, born in Dijon, France (d. 1621)
Catharina Questiers, Dutch poet (Friend of Abandoned Child), born in Amsterdam (d. 1669)
Johann Löhner, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1705)
Johann Michael Müller, German organist and composer, born in Schmalkalden, Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1743)
Carlo Innocenzo Fragoni, Italian poet, born in Genoa, Italy (d. 1768)
Jan Ekels the Elder, Amsterdam painter and cartoonist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1781)
Josiah Bartlett, American physician and statesman (signed US Declaration of Independence), born in Amesbury, Province of Massachusetts Bay (d. 1795)
Alessandro Felici, Italian composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1772)
Friedrich Schleiermacher, German theologist and philosopher, born in Breslau, Prussian Silesia (d. 1834)
Robert Brown, Scottish botanist (discovered 'Brownian Motion'), born in Montrose, Scotland (d. 1858)
Samuel Cunard, Canadian-British shipping magnate and founder (1st regular Atlantic steamship line), born in Halifax, Nova Scotia (d. 1865)
Richard B. Garnett, American Confederate Brigadier General, born in Essex County, Virginia (d. 1863)
Hieronymus Theodor Richter, German mineralogist and co-discoverer of the element indium, born in Dresden, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1898)
William McComb, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Mercer County, Pennsylvania (d. 1918)
Petrus Augustus de Génestet, Dutch vicar and poet, born in Amsterdam (d. 1861)
John Franklin Miller, American lawyer, businessman, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in South Bend, Indiana (d. 1886)
Joseph Jackson Bartlett, American attorney, diplomat and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Binghamton, New York (d. 1893)
Victoria, Empress of Germany and Queen of Prussia by marriage to German Emperor Frederick III, born in Buckingham Palace, London (d. 1901)
Désiré-Joseph Mercier, Belgian philosopher and cardinal of the Roman Catholic Church, born in Braine-l'Alleud, Belgium (d. 1926)
Francisco Tárrega, Spanish composer, born in Villarreal, Spain (d. 1909)
Hussein Kamel, Sultan of Egypt (1914-17), born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1917)
Manuel Estrada Cabrera, 13th President of Guatemala (1898-1920), born in Quetzaltenango, Guatemala (d. 1924)
Arthur Quiller-Couch, Cornish novelist and editor (The Oxford Book Of English Verse 1250–1900), born in Bodmin, Cornwall (d. 1944)
Albert Ward, English cricket batsman (7 Tests, 1 x 100, 3 x 50, HS 117; Yorkshire CCC), born in Leeds, England (d. 1939)
Vladimir Ipatieff, Russian-American chemist, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1952)
Joe Darling, Australian cricket batsman and captain (34 Tests, 21 as captain; 3 x 100s, TS 178; South Australia CA), born in Glen Osmond, Australia (d. 1946)
Sigfrid Edström, Swedish sports official (4th President International Olympic Committee 1952-64), born in Morlanda, Sweden (d. 1964)
Stanley Jackson, English cricket all-rounder (20 Tests, 5 x 100, HS 144no, 24 wickets; Yorkshire CCC, Cambridge University CCC), born in Leeds, England (d. 1947)
Panayot Pipkov, Bulgarian bandmaster, composer, pedagogue, and (with his son) Antarctic glacier namesake, born in Plovdiv, Bulgaria (d. 1942)
Louis Campbell-Tipton, American composer, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1921)
Sigfrid Karg-Elert, German composer, born in Oberndorf am Neckar, Germany (d. 1933)
Gustav Radbruch, German lawyer and jurist, born in Lübeck, GErmany (d. 1949)
Franz Hessel, German writer (Spazieren in Berlin (Walking in Berlin), poet, and translator (Marcel Proust), born in Szczecin, Poland (d. 1941)
Arthur Chesney [Kellaway], British stage and screen character actor (The Lodger; Chelsea Life), born in Wandsworth, London, England (d. 1949)
Harold Nicolson, English diplomat and author (Good Behaviour), born in Tehran, Persian Empire (d. 1968)
Corinne Griffith, American actress dubbed "The Orchid Lady of the Screen" (3 Hours, Lilies of Field), born in Texarkana, Texas (d. 1979)
Max Miller [Thomas Henry Sargent], English comedian known as The Cheeky Chappie, born in Brighton, England (d. 1963)
Mollie Steimer, American political prisoner and anarchist agitator, born in Dunaevtsy, Russia (d. 1980)
René Magritte, Belgian surrealist painter (This is Not a Pipe), born in Lessines, Belgium (d. 1967)
Jobyna Ralston, American actress (For Heaven's Sake), born in South Pittsburg, Tennessee (d. 1967)
Alice Calhoun, American silent film actress (Flowing Gold; Between Friends; Pampered Youth), and movie theater owner, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1966)
Foster Hewitt, Canadian radio broadcaster (Hockey Night in Canada), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1985)
Mikhail Suslov, Soviet statesman and party ideologist, born in Shakhovskoye, Russian Empire (d. 1982)
Coleman Hawkins, American jazz musician who virtually created tenor saxophone for jazz, born in St. Joseph, Missouri (d. 1969)
David Moule-Evans, English composer and conductor, born in Ashford, Kent, England (d. 1988)
Freddie Lindstrom, American Baseball Hall of Fame utility (New York Giants, Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1981)
Charles Korvin [Geza Korvin Karpathi], Austrian actor (Berlin Express, Ship of Fools), born in Piestany, Austria-Hungary (d. 1998)
Jim Bishop, American journalist and author (The Day Lincoln was Shot), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1987)
M Eleonore Lippits, 1st Dutch female missionary doctor, born in Hoorn, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Elizabeth George Speare, American author (The Witch of Blackbird Pond), born in Melrose, Massachusetts (d. 1994)
Anne Bolt, photo-journalist and trade unionist, born in London (d. 1996)
Eleanor Powell, American stage and screen actress and tap dancer (Born to Dance, Broadway Melody films), born in Springfield, Massachusetts (d. 1982)
Roy Boulting, British film director and producer (Desert Victory), born in Bray, Berkshire, Eng (d. 2001)

Sid Luckman, American College-Pro Football HOF quarterback (Columbia University; 4 × NFL C'ship 1940, 41, 43, 46; NFL MVP 1943; 5 × First-team All-Pro; 3 × Pro Bowl; Chicago Bears), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1998)
Sem Presser, Dutch press photographer, born in Amsterdam (d. 1986)
Eleanor Collins [Elnora Proctor], Canadian jazz and standards singer, and television personality, born in Edmonton, Alberta (d. 2024)
Steve Brodie [John Stevenson], American actor (The Life and Legend of Wyatt Earp), born in El Dorado, Kansas (d. 1992)
Ralph Meeker [Rathgeber], American actor (Mister Roberts, Anderson Tapes, Night Stalker), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1988)
Vivian Blaine, American actress (Guys & Dolls, Skirts Ahoy), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1995)
Abe Lemons, American basketball coach (Oklahoma City Uni, Pan American Uni; NABC Coach of the Year 1978 Uni of Texas Austin), born in Ryan, Oklahoma (d. 2002)
Maria Casares [Pérez], Spanish-French actress (Orpheus, Lectrice), born in A Coruña, Spain (d. 1996)
Christopher Tolkien, British author and son of J. R. R. Tolkien, born in Leeds, England (d. 2020)
Joseph Campanella, American character actor (Guiding Light, Mannix), born in New York City (d. 2018)
Milka Planinc, Prime Minister of Yugoslavia (1982-86), born in Drniš, Yugoslavia (d. 2010)
Poncke Princen, Dutch anti-Nazi fighter, colonial soldier and human rights activist, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 2002)
(Carlos) "Charlie" Palmieri, American salsa and charanga music bandleader, musical director, and piano player (Tito Puente; Alegre All-Stars), born in South Bronx, New York City (d. 1988)
Georgia Frontiere, American businesswoman (co-owner Los Angeles/St. Louis Rams 1979–2008), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 2008)
Laurier LaPierre, Canadian journalist, broadcaster and senator, born in Lac-Mégantic, Quebec (d. 2012)
Marilyn French, American author (The Women's Room), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2009)
Jim Ringo, American Pro Football HOF center (7 × First-team All-Pro; 10 × Pro Bowl; Green Bay Packers, Philadelphia Eagles) and coach (Buffalo Bills 1976–77), born in Orange, New Jersey (d. 2007)
Malcolm Williamson, Australian composer, born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2003)
Revaz Dogonadze, Georgian scientist (one of the founders of Quantum electrochemistry), born in Tbilisi, Georgia (d. 1985)
Pelle Gudmundsen-Holmgreen, Danish composer (Symphony and Antiphony), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2016)
Frank Brake, English entrepreneur (Brake Bros. Frozen Food), multi-millionaire, and philanthropist, born in Holborn. England (d. 2018)
Henry Hartsfield Jr, American astronaut (STS-4; STS-41-D; STS-61-A), born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 2014)
Jean Shepard, American country singer (Ozark Jubilee), born in Pauls Valley, Oklahoma (d. 2016)
Dietrich Weise, German soccer forward (Eintracht Frankfurt, FSV Mainz) and manager (FC Kaiserslautern, E Frankfurt, F Düsseldorf, Egypt, Liechtenstein), born in Gröben, Germany (d. 2020)
Fairuz [Nouhad Haddad], Lebanese singer ("Habbaytak Bissayf"; "Bektob Ismak Ya Habibi"), born in Zuqaq al-Blat, Beirut, State of Greater Lebanon
Laurence Luckinbill, actor (Delphi Bureau, Ike), born in Fort Smith, Arkansas
Peter Philpott, Australian cricket spin bowler (8 Tests, 26 wickets, best 5/90; NSW CA), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2021)
Robert Drivas [Choromokos], American stage and screen actor (Cool Hand Luke; Illustrated Man; Our Private World), and theater director, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1986)
James DePreist, American conductor (Mitropoulos Prize, 1964, Oregon Symphony, 1980-2003), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2013)
Victor Chang, Australian pioneering cardiac surgeon, born in Shanghai, China (d. 1986)
Marlo Thomas, American actress (That Girl; Jenny), and writer (Free to Be... You and Me), born in Detroit Michigan
R. Budd Dwyer, American politician who committed suicide on TV, born in St. Charles, Missouri (d. 1987)
Rick Lenz, actor (Hec Ramsey, Scandalous John), born in Springfield, Illinois
Amelia Freedman, British music impresario and founder of the Nash Ensemble of London, born in London (d. 2025)
Natalia Makarova, a Soviet-Russian-born prima ballerina and choreographer (Kirov Ballet, 1956-70), born in Leningrad, USSR
Richard Marcinko, American Navy Seal veteran and author, born in Lansford, Pennsylvania (d. 2021)

Terry Dischinger, American College Basketball HOF guard (Purdue; 3 x NBA All-Star; NBA Rookie of the Year 1963 Baltimore Bullets; Detroit Pistons; Olympic gold 1960), born in Terre Haute, Indiana (d. 2023)
Andrew Love, American saxophonist (The Memphis Horns), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 2012)
David Porter, American producer, arranger and songwriter ("Soul Man"; "Hold On, I'm Coming"), born in Memphis, Tennessee
İdil Biret, Turkish concert pianist, born in Ankara, Turkey
Juliet Mills, actress (Nanny & the Professor, QB VII), born in London, England
Afa Anoaʻi, Samoan-American WWE HOF pro wrestler (The Wild Samoans(with brother Sika): WWF Tag Team C'ship x 3), born in Territory of Western Samoa (d. 2024)
Heidemarie Wieczorek-Zeul, German politician of the Social Democratic Party, born in Frankfurt, Germany
Jacques Laffite, French auto racer (6 x F1 wins, Ligier) and broadcaster (TF1), born in Paris, France
Larry Mahan, American rodeo rider (PRCA All-Around Champion 1966–70, 73; ProRodeo Hall of Fame), born in Salem, Oregon (d. 2023)
Orlando de la Torre, Peruvian soccer defender (39 caps; Sporting Cristal SA), born in Trujillo, Peru (d. 2022)
Phil Bredesen, American businessman and politician (D), 48th Governor of Tennessee (2003-11), born in Oceanport, New Jersey
Robert Alner, British racehorse trainer (Cheltenham Gold Cup: Cool Dawn 1998), born in Droop, England (d. 2020)
Viktor Sidjak, Russian fencer (Olympic gold team sabre 1968, 76, 80; individual sabre 1972), born in Anzhero-Sudzhensk, Russia
Dick Durbin, American politician (Rep-D-IL, 1983-97, Senator-D-IL, 1997-), born in East St. Louis, Illinois
Earl "the Pearl" Monroe, American basketball HOF guard (4 x NBA All-Star; NBA C'ship 1973 NY Knicks; NBA Rookie of the Year 1968 Baltimore Bullets), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Harold Ramis, American actor and director (SCTV, Stripes), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2014)
Marcy Carsey, American TV executive who produced "The Cosby Show"  and "Roseanne", born in Weymouth, Massachusetts
Jacky Lafon, Belgian actress known for Flemish soap opera "Familie", born in Dendermonde, Belgium
Jónas Tómasson, Icelandic composer, teacher, and conductor, born in Ísafjörður, Westfjords, Iceland
Steve Webber, American baseball college coach (University of Georgia 1981-96; NCAA C'ship 1990), born in Stockport, Iowa (d. 2022)
Alphonse Mouzon, American jazz musician (Weather Report, Tenacious Records), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 2016)
George Zimmer, American businessman (Men's Warehouse), born in New York City
John "Rabbit" Bundrick, American keyboardist and organist (The Who, The Rocky Horror Picture Show), born in Houston, Texas
Leroy "Lonnie" Jordan, American rock keyboardist and vocalist (War - "Why Can't We Be Friends?"), born in San Diego, California
Mark Tulin, American psychedelic rock bassist (The Electric Prunes - "I Had Too Much to Dream (Last Night)"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2011)
Anatoliy Kuksov, Ukrainian soccer midfielder (8 caps USSR; FC Zorya Luhansk 489 games) and manager, born in Luhansk, Ukraine (d. 2022)
Barbara Jo Rubin, American jockey (first women to win a race on a major US track), born in Highland, Illinois
